channel.title: NYT Book Reviews (Filtered)
channel.description: Actual book reviews from the New York Times, filtered from the noisy Books RSS feed.

item.title: ‘Country People,’ by Daniel Mason
item.description: In Daniel Mason’s charming “Country People,” a stuck father and his happier family are tempted, and changed, during a stint on a bucolic campus.
item.author: Alexandra Jacobs

item.title: ‘American Trickster,’ by Ru Marshall
item.description: In “American Trickster,” Ru Marshall takes on the epic story of Carlos Castaneda.
item.author: Kurt Andersen

item.title: ‘Gerontocracy in America,’ by Samuel Moyn
item.description: A new book by Samuel Moyn argues that the country has become an “oldigarchy,” in which wealth and power are hoarded by the elderly.
item.author: Jennifer Szalai

item.title: ‘The Midnight Special,’ by Colin Asher
item.description: “The Midnight Special” offers an unconventional cultural history with profiles of five artists influenced by the carceral system.
item.author: Dwight Garner

item.title: ‘Dead but Dreaming of Electric Sheep,’ by Paul Tremblay
item.description: In Paul Tremblay’s new A.I. dystopia, “Dead but Dreaming of Electric Sheep,” an experimental technology gives its heroine control over somebody else’s body.
item.author: Emily C. Hughes

item.title: ‘Becoming George,’ by Fiona Sampson
item.description: In her biography of the novelist George Sand, Fiona Sampson urges us to see Sand chiefly as a writer, an inventor of stories and also of the self.
item.author: Rebecca Chao

item.title: ‘The Yahoo Boys,’ by Carlos Barragán
item.description: A new book examines the desperate lives of Nigeria’s romance scam artists — and their victims.
item.author: Nicolas Niarchos

item.title: ‘Lifeguard,’ by Janet Fash with Clio Chang
item.description: Janet Fash’s memoir is both a sunny coming-of-age story and an exposé of corruption, understaffing and unnecessary deaths at Rockaway Beach.
item.author: Carolyn Kormann

item.title: “Nebraska,” by Monica Datta
item.description: Monica Datta’s “Nebraska” is a maximalist, continent-spanning story of a mother killing her youngest child, as relayed by a highly idiosyncratic psychoanalyst.
item.author: Joumana Khatib

item.title: ‘The Housewives Underground,’ by Kaitlyn Tiffany
item.description: In “The Housewives Underground,” the Atlantic writer Kaitlyn Tiffany salutes a loose network of skeptics who questioned the findings of the Warren Report.
item.author: James Wolcott

item.title: ‘The Emergency Playbook,’ by Amy Edelman and Chris Begley
item.description: “The Emergency Playbook” is a disaster preparation guide that emphasizes community rather than lone-hero fantasies.
item.author: Dwight Garner

item.title: ‘The Reverse Centaur’s Guide to Life After AI,’ by Cory Doctorow
item.description: In “The Reverse Centaur’s Guide to Life After AI,” the renowned tech critic Cory Doctorow tries to find a good way to coexist with artificial intelligence.
item.author: Stephen Marche

item.title: ‘Regime Change,’ by Maggie Haberman and Jonathan Swan
item.description: In “Regime Change,” two New York Times journalists offer a riveting chronicle of the weird fusion of reality and show business in the White House.
item.author: Fintan O’Toole

item.title: ‘Ghost-Eye,’ by Amitav Ghosh
item.description: Fascinating if overstuffed, Amitav Ghosh’s “Ghost-Eye” connects the mystifying case of a girl in Calcutta to the global climate crisis.
item.author: Junot Díaz

item.title: ‘Presence: A Hidden History of the Female Body,’ by Erin Maglaque
item.description: In “Presence,” the historian Erin Maglaque pieces together the fragments of early modern womanhood.
item.author: Mattie Kahn

item.title: ‘The Frenzy,’ by Joyce Carol Oates
item.description: The stories in her new collection deal in jagged emergencies and in wounds both physical and psychic.
item.author: Rand Richards Cooper

item.title: ‘The Nord Stream Conspiracy,’ by Bojan Pancevski
item.description: In “The Nord Stream Conspiracy,” the investigative journalist Bojan Pancevski tells a high-stakes international war story in blockbuster prose.
item.author: Robert F. Worth

item.title: ‘Empire of Ink,’ by Alex Wright
item.description: In “Empire of Ink,” Alex Wright describes how newfangled technologies and disruptive personalities have regularly unsettled the American media.
item.author: Alexandra Jacobs

item.title: ‘Something We Said,’ by Elizabeth Stordeur Pryor
item.description: Elizabeth Stordeur Pryor’s new book, “Something We Said,” is at once a memoir and a history of a racial slur.
item.author: Lauretta Charlton

item.title: ‘They All Fall in Love at the End,’ by Haili Blassingame
item.description: In “They All Fall in Love at the End,” a young Black woman navigates taboo attractions and a contentious political environment while working toward a creative writing degree.
item.author: Alanna Bennett

item.title: ‘Drayton and Mackenzie,’ by Alexander Starritt
item.description: In “Drayton and Mackenzie,” two young grads navigating the 2008 financial crisis enact a plan they hope will change their fate.
item.author: John Williams

item.title: ‘Rialto,’ by Kate Milford, and ‘Fatal Glitch: Camp Zero,’ by Erin Entrada Kelly and Eliot Schrefer
item.description: Kate Milford’s cozy mystery is set at an abandoned amusement park, while Erin Entrada Kelly and Eliot Schrefer’s horror satire unfolds at a malevolent sleepaway camp.
item.author: Jennifer Hubert Swan

item.title: ‘As If,’ by Isabel Waidner
item.description: In this surreal book, two spiraling men swap lives.
item.author: Dustin Illingworth

item.title: ‘The American School of Spies,’ by Stephan Talty
item.description: In “The American School of Spies,” Stephan Talty tells the story of the desperate struggle to preserve antiquities during World War II.
item.author: Edward Dolnick

item.title: ‘This Dark Night: Emily Brontë, a Life,’ by Deborah Lutz
item.description: In her lyrical new biography, “This Dark Night,” Deborah Lutz shines light on the most enigmatic of the literary, secluded Brontë sisters.
item.author: Sadie Stein

item.title: ‘Cocked and Boozy,’ by Brooke Barbier
item.description: In her new history, “Cocked and Boozy,” Brooke Barbier illuminates the pervasive role that alcohol played throughout the colonial era.
item.author: J. D. Biersdorfer

item.title: ‘Red Sheet,’ by James Ellroy
item.description: Set in 1962 Los Angeles, “Red Sheet” follows the murder and mayhem behind a “mini Red Scare.”
item.author: William T. Vollmann

item.title: ‘Rasputin Swims the Potomac,’ by Ben Fountain
item.description: In Ben Fountain’s new novel, Washington insiders scheme to replace the president with a religious professional wrestler.
item.author: David Gates

item.title: ‘Trash!: A Garbageman’s Story,’ by Simon Paré-Poupart
item.description: In his memoir, Simon Paré-Poupart recounts the highs and lows of hauling trash for more than 20 years.
item.author: Dwight Garner

item.title: ‘Contrapposto,’ by Dave Eggers
item.description: In this satisfyingly old-school novel, an artist tries to find his place, and hold onto his spark, in a world that values fads and flash.
item.author: Alexandra Jacobs

item.title: ‘Earth 7,’ by Deb Olin Unferth
item.description: Deb Olin Unferth’s new novel is part cosmic comedy and part dirge for our dying world.
item.author: Lincoln Michel

item.title: ‘Villa Coco,’ by Andrew Sean Greer
item.description: Following his “Less” books with “Villa Coco,” Andrew Sean Greer drops an aimless postgraduate into a glamorous, romantic and secret-laden setting.
item.author: Stuart Emmrich

item.title: ‘The Man Who Stole the Gods,’ by Matthew Campbell
item.description: In “The Man Who Stole the Gods,” Matthew Campbell recounts a shocking, decades-long crime and the search for its perpetrator.
item.author: Hugh Eakin
